title: The American age was the human age
gist: Marking America's 250th anniversary, Noah Smith argues the U.S. will likely persist about as long as China's last three imperial dynasties (~270-290 years) but is sliding into local-veto-driven institutional sclerosis, hatred-organized politics, and collapsing patriotism, even as the real historical hinge — declining global fertility plus AI's rise — may be the end of the 'human age' itself, not just America's.

insights.claim: China's last three imperial dynasties lasted 268 (Qing), 276 (Ming), and 289 (Tang) years respectively, and on this historical base rate the 250-year-old United States will probably persist to a comparable age barring an abrupt catastrophe.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Sets a rough historical base-rate lifespan (~270-290 years) for the U.S. as a political order, implying the country has multiple more decades of runway before the kind of terminal institutional decay that preceded prior great powers' collapse.
insights.quote: will probably last as long as the Qing, the Ming, and probably the Tang.
insights.locator: China's last imperial dynasty, the Qing

insights.claim: Noah Smith frames his 250th-anniversary reflection around Senator Daniel Inouye's insistence that the Declaration of Independence's 33 founding words are 'operational' — a living creed, not just rhetoric — and uses that as the benchmark against which he judges whether America's founding creed still binds the country together.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Frames the whole essay's thesis test: whether the founding creed remains a functioning national glue or has become mere nostalgia, which bears on the durability of U.S. institutions over a long investment horizon.
insights.locator: "What other country would have done this?" — Daniel Inouye

insights.claim: Local veto power across much of the U.S. either blocks outright, delays by decades, or multiplies the cost of building factories, housing, energy, and transportation infrastructure relative to what other rich countries pay.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Explains the structural cost premium on U.S. physical build-out (housing, energy, transit) that keeps supply constrained — a durable headwind for any investment thesis that depends on America building things fast and cheap.
insights.quote: local veto power either prevents the construction of factories, housing, energy, transportation, and other infrastructure, or delays it by decades, or raises the cost to multiples of what other rich countries pay.
insights.locator: Much of the country has eased into a comfortable equilibrium of sclerosis

insights.claim: New industries can only get built in America today if they need almost no land, energy, or bank financing and can pull skilled labor from abroad as needed; the internet industry satisfied all of those conditions for about three decades, but Smith says that window has now closed.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Narrows the lane of what can still scale domestically to asset-light, low-energy, low-financing sectors (software, AI) — a filter for where U.S. growth and investable dynamism can still concentrate versus where sclerosis will keep blocking build-out.
insights.quote: Somehow the internet industry satisfied all of those conditions for three decades, but that time is done.
insights.locator: The industries of the future can only be built in America

insights.claim: Despite institutional sclerosis, U.S. GDP has stayed on the same smooth upward trend since measurement began roughly eight decades ago, and the AI industry specifically is 'upending the world' even as the country fails to build passenger rail and pays roughly double what other rich nations pay for health care.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Separates cyclical bearishness from structural strength: the growth engine (AI/tech) keeps compounding even as legacy public goods lag, a bifurcation relevant to sector positioning within U.S. equities.
insights.quote: our AI industry is upending the world
insights.locator: We cannot (or will not) build a functional passenger train network

insights.claim: American politics has degenerated into movements defined more by who they hate than by any positive vision: rightists are organized around hatred of immigration, leftists around hatred of Israel, and even intellectual liberals increasingly subordinate other goals to lowering the social status of wealthy technologists.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Diagnoses U.S. political dysfunction as identity-and-hatred-driven rather than policy-driven, which lowers the odds of coalition-building needed to fix the sclerosis problem — a soft signal for persistent gridlock risk.
insights.quote: Rightists are consumed by their hatred for immigration, leftists by their hatred for Israel.

insights.claim: Americans are now less proud of their nation than Europeans, Asians, or people in any other major world region — patriotism has fallen below the global average, per a July 2026 Nate Silver data callout that surprised even him.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: A collapse in national self-belief undercuts the collective willingness to fund and build the reforms needed to escape sclerosis — a soft indicator of long-run U.S. institutional cohesion risk.
insights.quote: But hadn't realized we're actually below the world average now.
insights.locator: We used to be an unusually patriotic nation

insights.claim: Neither major faction offers a workable positive vision: the right dreams of forcibly restoring the country's old ethnic composition, while the surging left within the Democratic party casts America as an evil empire 'straight from old Soviet propaganda' rather than proposing feasible reforms.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Suggests neither party's dominant narrative is capable of mobilizing the majority needed for the infrastructure and immigration fixes Smith says the sclerosis problem requires.
insights.quote: vision of America as an evil empire that could have come straight from old Soviet propaganda
insights.locator: The leftists who are surging among the Democrats

themes.theme: The end of the human age

insights.claim: Global fertility is falling below replacement everywhere while more of the world's thinking shifts from human brains into data centers, so it is unclear that a 'posthuman' world will even need the nation-state to play the organizing role it played over the last 250 years — making the real historical pivot point bigger than America's own fate: the end of the 'human age' itself.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Frames a long-horizon macro thesis: institutions built around nation-states may become structurally less load-bearing as AI-driven cognition and demographic decline reshape the basic unit of political organization — relevant to very long-duration sovereign-risk and demographic-linked positioning.
insights.quote: more and more of the thinking done on the planet will be done in data centers rather than within human brains.
insights.locator: The human race as a whole is set to dwindle
